plainMeaning: Tiny, barely-noticeable stirrings of your limbs, of phlegm in the throat, and of your eyes are also allowed and will not break your motionless meditation.
recitation: suhumehiṁ aṅga-saṁcālehiṁ, suhumehiṁ khela-saṁcālehiṁ, suhumehiṁ diṭṭhi-saṁcālehiṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit suhuma < Sanskrit sūkṣma ('fine, subtle'); instrumental plural ending -ehiṁ, agreeing with saṁcālehiṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term; here in the instrumental plural, governed by 'āgārehiṁ' in the next verse ('by these subtle exceptions').

wordByWord.gloss: by stirrings of the limbs / body
wordByWord.etymology: tatpuruṣa compound: aṅga ('limb, body-part') + saṁcāla ('slight movement') < Sanskrit saṁ-√cal ('to stir, shift'); instrumental plural -ehiṁ.
wordByWord.notes: saṁcāla is a glossary term; first member aṅga specifies the locus as the limbs.

wordByWord.etymology: Repetition of suhuma < sūkṣma; instrumental plural. The threefold repetition is anaphora, fixing 'subtle' on each saṁcāla in turn.
wordByWord.notes: Deliberate repetition for each of the three stirrings — not redundancy.

wordByWord.gloss: by stirrings of phlegm (in the throat/mouth)
wordByWord.etymology: tatpuruṣa: khela ('phlegm, mucus, saliva'; Prakrit khela, cf. Sanskrit kheṭa/śleṣman) + saṁcāla; instrumental plural -ehiṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Covers involuntary throat-clearing, swallowing, or shifting of phlegm — an internal, humoral stirring.

wordByWord.etymology: Third occurrence of suhuma < sūkṣma; instrumental plural, qualifying diṭṭhi-saṁcāla.

wordByWord.gloss: by stirrings of the gaze / eyes
wordByWord.etymology: tatpuruṣa: diṭṭhi ('sight, gaze'; Prakrit diṭṭhi < Sanskrit dṛṣṭi, √dṛś 'to see') + saṁcāla; instrumental plural -ehiṁ.
wordByWord.notes: The slight flicker or shift of the eyes/eyelids — the most subtle, sensory stirring of the three.

literalTranslation: By subtle stirrings of the limbs, by subtle stirrings of phlegm, by subtle stirrings of the gaze.
idiomaticTranslation: And likewise by the faint, involuntary stirrings of the body, by the slight movements of phlegm in the throat, and by the barely-perceptible flickers of the eyes — these too are permitted.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where the first verse named the gross, audible reflexes (breath, cough, sneeze, swoon), this verse descends to the threshold of perceptibility — the three suhuma saṁcāla. The thrice-repeated 'suhumehiṁ' is the verse's whole engine: by attaching 'subtle' to each item in turn, it tells the reciter that the concession of verse 1 reaches all the way down to motions one can scarcely feel oneself make. The three loci are chosen to span the body completely: aṅga (the outward frame — a finger that trembles, a muscle that twitches), khela (the inward, humoral channel — phlegm shifting, an involuntary swallow), and diṭṭhi (the sensory gate — the flicker of an eyelid or a drifting eye). Outer, inner, sensory: the triad is exhaustive by design, so that no honest reflex is left unaccounted for. Grammatically these instrumentals hang unfinished here; they are completed only in the next verse, where 'evamāiehiṁ āgārehiṁ' gathers verses 1–2 into a single phrase — 'by these and similar exceptions (the kāyotsarga remains unbroken).' Thus verse 2 is not a new sentence but the second tier of one list, refining the vow's honesty from the obvious to the imperceptible. The spiritual point is precise: stillness is defined by *intention*, not by the impossible suppression of every nerve. A flicker that the will did not command is no breach.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes the Jain distinction between willed action and involuntary motion as it bears on karmic bondage. Bandha follows from yoga energized by kaṣāya and intention; an aṅga-, khela-, or diṭṭhi-saṁcāla that arises below the level of volition carries no saṅkalpa and so does not violate the kāyotsarga's renunciation of bodily activity. By explicitly excusing the *subtlest* tier, the sutra guards the practice against scrupulosity — the false belief that an unbidden twitch is a transgression — and keeps the vow truthful rather than merely strict.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Standing in kāyotsarga, a reciter will inevitably feel a calf quiver, need to swallow, or sense the eyes drift. This verse is the standing instruction not to panic, 'correct,' or restart on their account: note them, let them pass, and keep the resolve of stillness. Effort is aimed at what the will controls; the involuntary is already forgiven.

crossReferences.text: Annattha Sūtra, v.1 (the gross āgāra) and v.3 ('evamāiehiṁ āgārehiṁ')
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Āvaśyaka Niryukti (kāyotsarga discipline and its permitted exceptions)
crossReferences.source: Āvaśyaka tradition, as cited in the corpus

sources.title: Annattha Sutra (Prakrit text, transliteration and meaning of the āgāra)
sources.consultedFor: verbatim text and standard English rendering of 'suhumehiṁ aṅga/khela/diṭṭhi-saṁcālehiṁ' as the three subtle stirrings

sources.title: Kayotsarga
sources.consultedFor: the discipline of motionlessness and the role of permitted involuntary acts in kāyotsarga

sources.title: Pratikramana
sources.consultedFor: placement of the kāyotsarga step within the six Āvaśyakas and the function of the Annattha exceptions

notes: Reading honored verbatim from the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) book. 'khela' is glossed across translators as phlegm/mucus (sometimes 'internal circulation/fluids'); the limbs–phlegm–gaze triad and the 'subtle' qualifier are stable across editions. The instrumental-plural endings (-ehiṁ) are syntactically completed only by 'āgārehiṁ' in v.3; this verse is grammatically dependent on that continuation. Some teaching traditions read khela-saṁcāla as covering swallowing/throat-clearing specifically — a gloss variation, not a textual one.
